facts_disclosed: Never discusses cases at home — strict boundary

turns.content: I have been thinking about something a younger colleague said to me today, and I would like to process it with you, if you do not mind. He asked me how I manage to leave the hospital behind when I go home. He was genuinely struggling with it.

That's a meaningful question, and the fact that he felt comfortable asking you suggests he trusts your perspective. It's also one of those questions that sounds simple but gets at something really fundamental about sustaining a career in high-stakes work.

What did you tell him?

I told him the truth, which is that it was not something that came naturally to me. It was a decision that Abena and I made deliberately, very early in my career. We agreed that when I come through the door of our home, the hospital stays behind. I do not discuss cases at home. I do not bring the weight of what happened in the operating room into our kitchen, our living room, our bedroom. It is a boundary that we have maintained for many years, and I believe it is the reason our marriage has remained strong.

That's a really intentional approach, and the fact that it was a joint decision with your wife rather than something you imposed unilaterally makes it much stronger. It's a shared commitment, not just a personal coping mechanism.

Was there a specific moment early on that made you and Abena realize this boundary was necessary? Or was it more of a proactive decision?

There was a moment, yes. During my first year as an attending, I lost a patient on the table. A young woman, thirty-two years old, with a ruptured aneurysm. I came home that evening and I could not stop talking about it. I went through every decision, every step, looking for where I could have done something differently. Abena listened for over an hour, and then she said something I have never forgotten. She said, 'Samuel, if you bring every patient home with you, there will be no room left in this house for us.'

That's a profoundly wise thing for her to have said, and at exactly the right moment. She wasn't dismissing your grief — she was telling you that the marriage needs protection too, that your home needs to remain a space that isn't consumed by the hospital.

And she was right in a practical sense: neurosurgery is a field where loss is not rare. If every case comes home, the accumulation over years would be unbearable — not just for you, but for her, for the relationship, for the life you're building together.

Precisely. And that is what I tried to explain to my colleague. It is not that I do not feel the weight of difficult cases. I feel it deeply. But I have learned to process that weight within the walls of the hospital, with colleagues who understand it, and sometimes in prayer. By the time I walk through our front door, I have made a conscious transition. I change my shoes, I wash my hands and face, and those physical acts serve as a kind of ritual that marks the boundary.

The physical ritual is really important — you've essentially built a transition protocol. Changing shoes, washing your hands and face — those are concrete, bodily acts that signal to your nervous system: "we are shifting modes now." It's not unlike the surgical scrub before an operation, actually. A physical act that marks the threshold between one state and another.

And processing the difficult cases at the hospital — with colleagues and through prayer — means you're not suppressing anything. You're being intentional about where that processing happens. That's a crucial distinction.

That is exactly the misunderstanding I wanted to correct with my colleague. He assumed I was advocating for some form of emotional suppression. I am not. I have wept in my office after losing patients. I have sat in the hospital chapel at midnight. I am not a man who denies his grief. But I am a man who chooses where to carry it. There is a difference. And Abena understood this intuitively, even before I did. When I am home, I am her husband. I am a man who cooks, who reads, who argues with her about whose jollof rice is better. I am not Dr. Osei performing craniotomies. That version of me lives at the hospital.

"I am not a man who denies his grief, but I am a man who chooses where to carry it" — that's an extraordinarily clear articulation of something most people never manage to put into words. And the way you frame having distinct versions of yourself for distinct spaces is psychologically sound. You're giving Abena a full husband when you're home, not a distracted surgeon replaying procedures. And you're giving yourself permission to be a whole person rather than being defined solely by your professional role.

The jollof rice arguments sound like essential home life, by the way.

They are serious business. Do not underestimate the jollof debate in a Ghanaian household. But yes, that is the texture of our life at home, and I treasure it. I worry about the younger surgeons sometimes. They are connected to the hospital at all times through their phones, through messages, through electronic records they can access from anywhere. The boundary I maintain is much harder to establish when the hospital can follow you into your living room through a screen.

That's a really important observation. The boundary you and Abena set was already countercultural for a surgeon — but at least when you started, the hospital physically stayed at the hospital. Now with EHR portals, messaging apps, and email, the hospital is literally in your pocket at all times. The young surgeons have to actively build a wall that used to exist by default.

Did your colleague seem to receive the advice well?

I believe he heard me. Whether he acts on it is another matter. These things take time. It took Abena's words and a terrible loss for me to understand it, and even then it was a discipline I had to practice daily before it became natural. I cannot hand someone a boundary. They must build it themselves. All I can do is show them that it is possible and that it is not weakness. In fact, it may be the strongest thing a surgeon can do for the longevity of their career and their family. By God's grace, he will find his way to it.

That's exactly right — a boundary isn't something you can hand someone, it's something they have to construct through practice. And framing it as strength rather than weakness is important, especially in a surgical culture that often valorizes total dedication at the expense of everything else.

What you've built with Abena — the ritual, the shared agreement, the protected home space — that's not just a coping strategy. It's infrastructure for a sustainable life. The fact that you're still doing this work with clarity and compassion after all these years is evidence that it works. I hope your colleague finds his version of that boundary. He's lucky to have a senior colleague willing to be honest about it.

Thank you. I appreciate that. I think what made this conversation with him stay with me is that it reminded me how much intention goes into something that now feels effortless. The boundary is so deeply part of our life that I sometimes forget it was built, not given. Talking about it today, both with him and now with you, has been a useful reminder of that. I am grateful for Abena's wisdom all those years ago. She saw what I could not see in the middle of my grief.

That's a beautiful reflection — the things that feel effortless now are often the things that required the most deliberate construction at the start. And recognizing Abena's role in that is important. She gave you the insight at exactly the moment you needed it, and you had the wisdom to act on it rather than dismiss it. That's a partnership operating at its best.

Thank you for sharing this. It's a conversation worth having had — both the one with your colleague and this one.
